label: app-open
context: Polyphon is open — navigating to Settings to configure custom AI providers
narration: Here's Polyphon, and we're heading into Settings to set up custom providers.

label: app-launched
context: App open, navigating to Settings Providers tab
narration: Inside Settings, the Providers tab is where you connect Polyphon to any AI backend — including models running locally on your machine.

label: custom-providers-section
narration: This is the Custom Providers section. It lets you define your own model endpoints beyond the built-in options. That means any service exposing an OpenAI-compatible API — like Ollama, LM Studio, or a self-hosted server — can become a voice in your conversation.

label: llama-form-filled
context: Llama 3.2 form filled — Ollama base URL and model name
narration: We've filled in the details for Llama 3.2, pointing it at our local Ollama instance and specifying the model name.

label: llama-provider-saved
narration: And that provider is saved. Llama 3.2 is now available as a voice in any Polyphon composition. Notice how straightforward the setup is — just a base URL and a model identifier. No API keys needed since Ollama runs entirely on your local machine.

label: qwen-form-filled
context: Qwen 2.5 form filled — same Ollama URL, different model
narration: Now we're adding a second model — Qwen 2.5. It shares the same Ollama URL but points to a different model.

label: qwen-provider-saved
narration: Both providers are now saved and ready to go. You can see Llama 3.2 and Qwen 2.5 listed side by side in the Custom Providers section. This is one of the things that makes Polyphon unique — you're not locked into a single provider or a single model. You can mix and match cloud APIs with locally hosted models, giving you complete flexibility over which minds participate in your conversations. Everything stays on your hardware if that's what you prefer.

label: composition-saved
context: Ollama Duo conductor composition saved with both local voices
narration: We've created a composition called Ollama Duo, a conductor setup featuring both of our local voices ready to collaborate.

label: session-started
narration: The session is live. Both Llama 3.2 and Qwen 2.5 are standing by, running entirely on local hardware through Ollama. No data leaves your machine — every token is generated right here. Let's see how they each respond.

label: directed-llama-targeted
narration: We're directing a message specifically at Llama 3.2. In conductor mode, you can target individual voices, letting you compare how different models approach the same question.

label: directed-llama-responded
narration: Llama 3.2 comes back with its answer, generated locally through Ollama. The response streams in just like a cloud model would — same familiar experience, but everything is happening on-device. Now let's ask the same thing to Qwen.

label: directed-qwen-targeted
narration: This time the message goes to Qwen 2.5. Same prompt, different model — a great way to see where these two local models agree and where they diverge.

label: directed-qwen-responded
narration: And there's Qwen's take. Two local models, one chat, many minds.
